coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence immunoassay based on the inner-filter effect of carbon dots for highly sensitive amantadine detection in foodstuffs
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Baolei
Li, Hongfang
Mujtaba Mari, Ghulam
Yu, Xuezhi
Yu, Wenbo
Wen, Kai
Ke, Yuebin
Shen, Jianzhong
Wang, Zhanhui
coredata.dc:description: Immunoassays with ultra-high sensitivity for the rapid detection of chemical contaminants in food are urgently required. However, conventional enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) usually suffer from the moderate sensitivity. Herein, we aim to improve the sensitivity of conventional ELISA by employing the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as the signal probes based on the principle of inner filter effect (IFE). In this strategy, the enzymatically formed products of horseradish peroxidase/alkaline phosphatase efficiently quenched the CDs via the IFE. The absorption signal of the conventional ELISA was converted into the fluorescence signal. The fluorescent immunoassay was successfully developed and used to detect amantadine residues in chicken, achieving a limit of detection of 0.02 ng mL−1. The fluorescent immunoassay is a straightforward, extendable and general strategy and exhibits potential in detecting trace amounts of chemical contaminants in foodstuff.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Fluorescent immunoassay
Ultra-sensitivity
Amantadine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The IFE-based fluorescence quenching of CDs
Development of the CDs-based fluorescent ELISA
Evaluation of the IFE between the CDs and the HRP/ALP substrates
Characterization of IFE-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs
Analytical performance of the CDs-based fluorescent ELISA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The excitation/emission spectra; TEM, HRTEM images and size distributions of CDs: (a) CD1 (b) CD2 (c) CD3 (d) CD4; The maximum excitation/emission, lattice spacing and mean diameter of CDs were (a) 360 nm/445 nm, 0.34 nm, 4.5 nm for CD1; (b) 495 nm/585 nm, 0.24 nm, 5.7 nm for CD2; (c) 380 nm/530 nm, 0.34 nm, 3.5 nm for CD3; (d) 415 nm/525 nm, 0.34 nm, 3.2 nm for CD4. The absolute quantum yields (QY) of four CDs aqueous solution were 60.6%, 46.2%, 23.4% and 26.2% respectively (Table S1).

The prerequisite selection of CDs and substrates for efficient IFE: (a) The study of fluorescence emission interference by comparing the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and TMB/TMBox. (b) Fluorescence excitation spectra of the CDs and the absorption spectra of the HRP substrate. (c) Fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of the ALP substrate. (d) Fluorescence lifetime of CD3 before (red, 7.04 ns) and after (blue, 7.09 ns) the quenching caused by IFE; (e) Fluorescence lifetime of CD4 before (red, 5.19 ns) and after (blue, 5.11 ns) the quenching caused by IFE. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Plots of F0 − F values versus the enzyme concentrations. (a) HRP concentration from 0 to 2.5 mU mL−1 (inset: its corresponding linear relationship from 0 to 0.4 mU mL−1; Y = 4538.33*X + 387.86, R2 = 0.989). The fluorescence was measured under excitation/emission of 370 nm/530 nm. (b) ALP concentration from 0 to 40 U/L (inset: its corresponding linear relationship from 0 to 5.0 mU mL−1; Y = 186.57*X + 74.43, R2 = 0.990). The fluorescence was measured with excitation/emission at 405 nm/525 nm. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of enzymes, respectively. Quantitative detection of AMD using (c) the HRP-based colorimetric ELISA and fluorescent ELISA; (d) the ALP-based colorimetric ELISA and fluorescent ELISA; (all the detection were performed in buffer containing 8% methanol, n = 3).
(a) The principle of IFE-based fluorescence quenching of CDs; (b) Schematic illustration of the CDs-based fluorescent ELISA for the detection of AMD.
An overview on recently reported immunoassays for the detection of AMD.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cut-off value was defined as the minimum concentration of AMD to produce invisible test lines in LFIA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mean recoveries for detecting AMD residues in chicken (n = 6).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: F-ELISA is the abbreviation of fluorescent ELISA.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence immunoassay based on the inner-filter effect of carbon dots for highly sensitive amantadine detection in foodstuffs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, China Agricultural University, Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety and Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety, 100193 Beijing, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health
Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety and Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, China Agricultural University, Beijing Key Laboratory of Detection Technology for Animal-Derived Food Safety and Beijing Laboratory for Food Quality and Safety, 100193 Beijing, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Molecular Epidemiology, Shenzhen Center for Disease Control and Prevention, 518020 Shenzhen, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Genetic Toxicology, Shenzhen Center for Disease Control and Prevention, 518020 Shenzhen, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of a highly sensitive CDs-based fluorescent ELISA. The enzymatically formed products of horseradish peroxidase and alkaline phosphatase efficiently quenched the fluorescence of CDs via the IFE, realize the signal conversion from absorbance to fluorescence.

Immunoassays with ultra-high sensitivity for the rapid detection of chemical contaminants in food are urgently required. However, conventional enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) usually suffer from the moderate sensitivity. Herein, we aim to improve the sensitivity of conventional ELISA by employing the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as the signal probes based on the principle of inner filter effect (IFE). In this strategy, the enzymatically formed products of horseradish peroxidase/alkaline phosphatase efficiently quenched the CDs via the IFE. The absorption signal of the conventional ELISA was converted into the fluorescence signal. The fluorescent immunoassay was successfully developed and used to detect amantadine residues in chicken, achieving a limit of detection of 0.02 ng mL−1. The fluorescent immunoassay is a straightforward, extendable and general strategy and exhibits potential in detecting trace amounts of chemical contaminants in foodstuff.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is a most frequently used screening method and often acts as the gold standard in the rapid detection of chemical contaminants in food samples (Wang, Beier, & Shen, 2017). A conventional ELISA uses enzymes, typically horseradish peroxidase (HRP) or alkaline phosphatase (ALP) to catalyze the enzyme substrate into colored products. The conventional ELISA has lower sensitivity derived from the lower signal to noise (S/N) ratio, making it unsuitable for detecting the trace levels of chemical contaminants in foodstuffs. A feasible and frequently employed strategy is to replace absorption signals by fluorescence counterpart to achieve higher S/N ratio and sensitivity (Barroso, Saa, Grinyte, & Pavlov, 2016; Gouverneur & Hughes, 2017; Zhu et al., 2019). Recently, fluorescent nanomaterials (like quantum dots (QDs), lanthanides and upconversion nanoparticles) have been incorporated into the ELISA platform to establish fluorescent immunoassays (Grinyte, Barroso, Möller, Saa, & Pavlov, 2016; Hlavacek et al., 2017). However, most of the developed fluorescent immunoassays typically employ fluorescent nanomaterials as signal labels for antibodies (known as a fluorescence-linked immunosorbent assay, FLISA) (Goftman et al., 2016). Such strategy sacrifices the signal amplification of enzymatic reactions, usually resulting in comparable sensitivity to that of ELISA. In order to obtain higher sensitivity, a new fluorescent immunoassay should have the advantages of both enzymatic amplification and fluorescent signal readout. Several fluorescent enzyme immunoassays employ special substrates and enzymes, thus, limiting their wide application (Sun et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2016). A generic fluorescent immunoassay which directly takes advantage of the commercially available enzymes (HRP or ALP) is more promising and practical.

Carbon dots (CDs) usually refer to carbon nanocrystals of less than 10 nm in size, exhibit a high quantum yield, good chemical and photo-stability, simple synthesis and good biocompatibility (Baker & Baker, 2010). Compared with QDs, CDs are less toxic and do not contain toxic heavy metal elements (Pb, Cd, etc.) (Jian et al., 2017). Due to these advantages, CDs have been increasingly used in food analysis by directly using the photoluminescence (PL) quenching of CDs, which lack sensitivity or specificity (Hu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016). Hence, the combination of CDs and immunoassays with high specificity derived from an antibody showed exciting potential in achieving highly sensitive and specific detection. In this study, we introduced CDs into ELISA system via the inner filter effect (IFE) to develop a novel fluorescent ELISA. The IFE is a non-irradiation energy conversion and occurs in a fluorescent assay system when the excitation or emission light of the fluorescent probe is absorbed by the absorbent in the matrix (Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018). This absorption caused by the matrix reduces the fluorescence intensity of the probes. The IFE has been proved to be an efficient and powerful strategy for developing novel fluorescent assays by converting absorption responses to fluorescence signals (Shang & Dong, 2009; Zhang et al., 2012). It would therefore be more flexible to design the IFE-based fluorescent assay by selecting and pairing different absorbents and probes (Li et al., 2016; Chen, Zhao, Hu, Sun, & Yang, 2017). However, an IFE-based immunoassay has not yet been frequently reported.

Amantadine (AMD) was once widely used in the poultry industry due to its efficient antiviral activity. However, illegal abuse of AMD can have serious consequences on human health, such as nausea, dizziness and the occurrence of AMD-resistant strains (Dong et al., 2015). Due to these concerns, the use of AMD as an antiviral drug in the poultry industry has been prohibited in many countries including China since 2005 (Peng et al., 2017). Thus, the establishment of a sensitive, rapid and accurate method for the detection of AMD residues was necessary (Wu, Zhu, et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018). Herein, we used CDs as the signal probe to develop a CDs-based fluorescent ELISA for the detection of AMD. The novel fluorescent ELISA demonstrated outstanding specificity and reproducibility, and significantly improved sensitivity compared with the conventional ELISA using the same immunoreagents. Moreover, the strategy we established was based on the conventional HRP/ALP ELISA system, which means that it can be conveniently extended to detect other chemical contaminants in food quality control.
Details regarding the materials and apparatus are provided in Supporting Information.
The detail procedures were described in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The IFE-based fluorescence quenching of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The IFE-based fluorescence quenching of CDs using HRP was performed using the following procedure: HRP with activity ranging from 0 mU mL−1 to 2.5 mU mL−1 was added to 1 mL mixed 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) substrate. The reaction solution was incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. Then 100 μL of the mixture was transferred to a microtiter plate followed by the addition of 20 μL diluted CD3 solution (10 μg mL−1). The fluorescence was measured following incubation for 1 min. The quenching of CDs using ALP was performed using a similar procedure (ALP activity ranging from 0 mU mL−1 to 40 mU mL−1). The mixture of ALP and 4-nitrophenyl phosphate (pNPP) substrate was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then 100 μL of the mixture was transferred to a microtiter plate followed by the addition of 20 μL diluted CD4 solution (10 μg mL−1). The fluorescence was measured following incubation for 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of the CDs-based fluorescent ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of coating antigen BSA-AMD (100 μL well−1) in a carbonate buffer (CB) were added to 96-well microtiter plates and incubated overnight at 4 °C. The plates were washed twice with PBST (0.2% tween 20 in phosphate-buffered saline, PBS, pH = 7.4) and blocked with 150 μL of 2% casein in PBS and then incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h. After three subsequent washing steps, a series of 50 μL well–1AMD standard solutions (or samples solution) and the same volume of diluted monoclonal antibody (mAb) were added to each well, respectively. Following incubation at 37 °C for 0.5 h, the plates were washed with PBST three times and 100 μL of HRP-labeled anti-mouse IgG (diluted 1:5000; or ALP-labeled anti-mouse IgG, diluted 1:2500) was added to each well and then incubated for another 0.5 h at 37 °C. After washing the plates three times, 100 μL well−1 of the mixed TMB substrate (or pNPP substrate) and 20 μL well−1 of CD3 solution (or CD4 solution, respectively, both 10 μg mL−1) were added to each well of the microtiter plates. The enzymatic reaction lasted for 15 min (or 25 min for the ALP-based fluorescent ELISA). The fluorescence was measured with excitation/emission at 370/530 nm for HRP-based fluorescent ELISA (or excitation/emission at 405/525 nm for ALP-based fluorescent ELISA).

Blank chicken samples were provided by the Veterinary Drug Safety Inspection and Testing Center of the Ministry of Agriculture (Beijing, China). Sample pretreatment is similar to our previous report (Ma et al., 2018). Two grams of the AMD-spiked samples was homogenized, and then placed in a centrifuge tube. Then 0.2 mL of 1% (v/v) glacial acetic acid aqueous solution and 2 mL methanol (80%) were added and the mixture was vortex mixed for 5 min and then centrifuged for 5 min at 8000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted 10-fold with PBS and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane for fluorescent ELISA analysis. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the mean value of 20 blank samples plus three times the mean standard deviation.

Scheme 1 outlines the proposed CDs-based fluorescent ELISA for the detection of AMD. The principle of IFE-based fluorescence quenching of CDs is shown in Scheme 1a. Briefly, HRP catalyzed the oxidation of colorless TMB and then generated the blue and light-absorbing substance TMBox in the presence of H2O2. The efficient IFE occurred between TMBox and CDs as the absorption spectra of TMBox overlaps the fluorescent excitation spectra of the CDs. The excitation light of the CDs during the PL was absorbed by TMBox due to its high molar absorption coefficient. Occurrence of the IFE reduced the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. In our proposed strategy, the decrease in IFE-based fluorescence was directly correlated with TMBox generated by the commonly used pair HRP/TMB in conventional ELISA. The detection of AMD was performed in 96-well microplates (Scheme 1b). The procedure was similar to that of the conventional ELISA except that the mixture of substrate (TMB and H2O2) and CDs was added to the wells after completion of the immunoreaction. As shown in Scheme 1b, HRP labeled secondary antibody catalyzed the oxidation of TMB into TMBox which quenched the fluorescence of the CDs via the IFE. Taking advantage of the efficient IFE between TMBox and CDs, the absorption signal of TMBox in the conventional ELISA was converted into a fluorescence signal. A similar principle also existed in the ALP-based fluorescent ELISA (Fig. S1). The ALP catalyzed the hydrolysis of pNPP. The yellow reaction product pNP also quenched fluorescent CDs via the IFE.

The fluorescence spectral characteristics as well as the fluorescence stability of CDs are key influencing factors for the IFE between the CDs and substrates. Four CDs with fluorescence at blue, yellow, and red wavelengths were synthesized and characterized for selection. The common and convenient hydrothermal and microwave-assisted methods are chosen for synthesizing CDs (Zheng, Ananthanarayanan, Luo, & Chen, 2015). In order to select matched pairs of CDs and absorbents to achieve an effective IFE, four citric acid-derived CDs with different excitation and emission spectra were synthesized and characterized. CD1 was synthesized by the hydrothermal method using water as the solvent. Citric acid acted as the carbon source; urea acted as the nitrogen sources. CD1 showed strong blue emission under ultraviolet (UV) excitation. The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of CD1 aqueous solution were 360 nm/445 nm. Fig. 1 a showed the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs of monodisperse CD1. The high density of amino-groups on the surface of CD1 can introduce surface traps, making CD1 emit blue fluorescence under ultraviolet excitation (Zhu, Zhao, Song, Lu, & Yang, 2016). CD2 was also synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea, using DMF instead of water as the solvent. The formation of CD2 with a large sized conjugated sp2–domain and high extent of graphitization was the basis of orange emission. Both the maximum excitation and emission wavelength had a large red-shift and were located at 495 nm/605 nm, respectively. The TEM images illustrate that CD2 was also monodispersed (Fig. 1b). CD3 and CD4 were prepared using the microwave-assisted method where citric acid acted as the carbon source; urea and thiourea acted as the nitrogen sources, respectively. Both CD3 and CD4 exhibited strong green emission under UV excitation. The edge states (a special surface state, consisting of the surface functional carboxyl groups and carbonyl groups on the edge of the carbon backbone) of the CDs were the common origin of green emission (Wang et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 1c and 1d, the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of the CD3 and CD4 aqueous solutions were 380 nm/530 nm and 415 nm/525 nm, respectively. The TEM images also confirmed the monodispersity of CD3 and CD4 (Fig. 1c and d). The size distribution and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of these four CDs are shown in Fig. 1a–d. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) information of these CDs is shown in Fig. S2. The characterization data were similar to those in previously reported studies, indicating successful synthesis of the four CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the IFE between the CDs and the HRP/ALP substrates
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we aimed to use CDs to achieve signal transition and amplification for ELISA via the IFE. CDs functioned as the fluorescent probes and the catalytic products of HRP and ALP (TMBox and pNP) as absorbents. The HRP substrate (pH = 4.5) consists of H2O2 (10 mM) and TMB (2.5 mM), and the ALP substrate (pH = 9.6) consists of pNPP (5 mM) and Mg2+ (0.01 mM, a cofactor for ALP). TMBox and pNP exhibited their maximal absorption wavelengths at 370 nm (another weaker absorption peak of TMBox was located at 652 nm) and 405 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). Both TMBox and pNP are stable and have large extinction coefficients which resulted in them being good absorbents in the IFE experiments (Jiang et al., 2016; Liu, Li, Xia, & Ren, 2016). We first investigated the fluorescent interaction between CDs and TMBox/pNP in solution during the IFE. One of the prerequisites for IFE-based signal conversion and amplification is that the detection system has neither background fluorescence nor strong interfering fluorescence (Chang & Ho, 2015). Thus, the signal changes in the fluorescent probes (CDs) could accurately reflect the changes in the target analyte concentration. The HRP substrate consisted of H2O2 and TMB. The unsaturated ring system of TMB emitted strong fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength of 430 nm under UV excitation (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S3a). This strong fluorescence was related to the resonance interaction at different positions of the molecules as well as the formation of the quinoid structure with a planar configuration in the excited state (Yang, Wang, Xiao, Luo, & Yang, 2000). As shown in Fig. 2a, CD1 showed maximum emission at 445 nm. The fluorescence of TMB markedly disturbed the output signals of CD1 during the IFE; therefore, CD1 was abandoned in the next HRP- and IFE-based signal-transition experiments. Both pNPP and pNP were non-fluorescent. The fluorescent outputs of all four CDs were not influenced by pNPP and pNP in solution.

In addition, the IFE would only effectively occur if the absorption spectra of the absorbents overlapped the fluorescent excitation or emission spectra of the CDs (Chen et al., 2018). The excitation light of the CDs PL was weakened by the absorbents leading to a decline in emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 2b, the maximum excitation wavelengths of CD2 and CD4 were 495 nm and 415 nm, respectively. The small overlapping parts between the excitation spectra of CD2, CD4 and the absorption spectra of TMBox (maximum absorption wavelength was 370 nm) led to a weak IFE. CD2 and CD4 were unsuitable for the HRP-based IFE. The maximum excitation wavelength of CD3 was located at 380 nm, which was very close to the maximal absorption peak of TMBox. The fluorescence of CD3 could be efficiently quenched by TMBox via the IFE. Thus, CD3 was a suitable fluorescent probe for the HRP-based IFE. According to Fig. 2c, the maximum absorption wavelength of pNP was located at 405 nm where it covered most of the emission spectra of CD3 and CD4. Thus, CD3 and CD4 were applied in the subsequent ALP-based IFE.

The fluorescence stability of CDs during the measurement procedures is another important factor for an efficient IFE. According to previously reported studies, the fluorescence of CDs is sensitive to pH, oxidants, etc, (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). Thus, the fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by the matrix of the detection system (not by the HRP/ALP-catalyzed products) may result in signal interference. To avoid the signal interference caused by the matrix, the fluorescence stability of the CDs in the matrix was systematically evaluated. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of CD3 and CD4 aqueous dispersions at different pH values are shown in Figs. S4a and S5a. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CD3 gradually increased within the pH range of 1– 5, was maximum at pH = 5 and then dropped by half at pH = 10, while the fluorescence stability of CD4 was good in the pH range of 3 to 9. Taking into consideration the optimal pH values for HRP activity (from 3 to 5) and ALP activity (from 8 to 10), CD3 and CD4 were acceptable for acting as the probes in the HRP-and ALP-triggered IFE, respectively. The effects of the main component of the enzyme substrates on the fluorescence intensity of CD3 and CD4 were also evaluated. The addition of H2O2 did not quench the fluorescence of CD3 until the concentration of H2O2 was above 100 mM, which was much higher than the actual H2O2 concentration in HRP substrate (Fig. S4b). As shown in Fig. S5b, the fluorescence intensity of CD4 did not decrease as the concentration of pNPP increased from 0 mM to 40 mM. The results in Fig. S6 indicated that both TMB and Mg2+ showed no obvious interference on the fluorescence of CD3 and CD4, respectively. Due to the excellent photo-stability, the fluorescence of CD3 and CD4 during the IFE process was also very stable within 25 min (Figs. S4c and S5c). These results confirmed that CD3 and CD4 were suitable fluorescent probes for IFE-based signal transition and amplification. It has been reported that some CDs have enzyme-mimicking properties (such as peroxidase-mimicking activity) (Vazquez-Gonzalez et al., 2017). The CDs which exhibit catalytic performance could catalyze the oxidization of some substrates (TMB, luminol, etc) in the presence of H2O2. The potential catalytic abilities of the CDs are interferences for the enzyme-triggered IFE. In order to prove that CD3 and CD4 had no catalytic abilities, the optical density (OD) values of HRP and ALP substrates containing 10 µg mL−1 CD3 and CD4 were recorded, respectively. As shown in Fig. S7, there were no obvious OD changes for either HRP or ALP substrates within 10 min. These results indicated that CD3 and CD4 had no catalytic ability for HRP and ALP substrates, respectively. The above results confirmed that the selected CD3 and CD4 were stable fluorescent probes during the HRP/ALP-triggered and IFE-based signal transition and amplification, respectively.

We conducted fluorescence lifetime assay and ultrafiltration to confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 2d and e, both the lifetimes of CD3 and CD4 were unaffected by the presence of TMBox and pNPP, respectively. The ultrafiltration separated the CDs from the quenchers, leading to recovered fluorescence of the CDs (Fig. S8), indicating that no non-fluorescent complexes were formed. From the above results, we could conclude that the fluorescence quenching of CD3/CD4 was due to the IFE instead of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or static quenching mechanism (Zu et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2015; Wu, Song, et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of IFE-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: After determining the principles of IFE-based CDs fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence variations of the CDs in relation to the different concentrations of HRP/ALP were investigated. The fluorescence changes of CD3/CD4 during the IFE were recorded following the addition of different concentrations of HRP (from 0 to 2.5 mU mL−1) or ALP (from 0 to 40 mU mL−1). As shown in Fig. 3 a, a linear relationship was obtained between the relative fluorescence intensity of CD3 (F0 − F) and the concentration of HRP in the range of 0 to 0.40 mU mL−1. The fitted linear equation in Fig. 4a inset was: Y = 4538.33*X + 387.86, (R2 = 0.989). Similarly, as seen in Fig. 3b, a good linear relationship between ALP concentration (from 0 to 5.0 mU mL−1) and CD4 fluorescence intensity change (F0 − F) was observed. The linear fitting equation in Fig. 4b inset could be expressed as follows: Y = 186.57*X + 74.43, (R2 = 0.990). Marked sensitive detection of HRP (ALP) activity at 0.003 mU mL−1 (0.01 mU mL−1) was achieved using the developed fluorescent sensing platform (S/N = 3). Compared with the colorimetric method (LOD of HRP/ALP activity was 0.03 mU mL−1/0.5 mU mL−1, Fig. S9), the fluorescent assay based on the IFE had higher sensitivity for the detection of HRP/ALP activity, which is fundamental for constructing a fluorescent ELISA with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the CDs-based fluorescent ELISA
ce:section.ce:para: After selecting matched pairs of CDs and absorbents for an efficient IFE, we developed a CDs-based fluorescent ELISA for the detection of AMD. The following parameters of the fluorescent ELISA were optimized: concentrations of coating antigen and mAb, incubating time and the matrix effect of the sample extract. Respective data and Figures are given in the Supporting Information. In short, the following experimental conditions were found to give best results: (a) the optimum working concentrations of BSA-AMD and anti-AMD mAb were 3.3 × 10−5 mg mL−1 (2.5 × 10−5 mg mL−1) and 3.6 × 10−5 mg mL−1 (4.8 × 10−5 mg mL−1) for the HRP-based fluorescent ELISA (or ALP-based fluorescent ELISA), respectively (Table S2). (b) The optimum incubating time was 25 min/15 min for HRP/ALP-based fluorescent ELISA (Fig. S10a). (c) The spiked chicken samples were extracted with 80% methanol and then diluted 10-fold to remove the matrix effect (Fig. S10b).

Under the optimized conditions, the standard curves of the fluorescent ELISA based on HRP/ALP were established. The F0–F values were plotted against the logarithm of concentrations of the AMD (0–6 ng mL−1), where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the blank well (absence of mAb) and AMD-spiked wells, respectively. The lowest inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the fluorescent ELISA using HRP (or ALP) was 0.3 ng mL−1 (or 0.41 ng mL−1), which was approximately 4-fold lower than the conventional ELISA (IC50 = 1.1 ng mL−1), Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d. The LOD of the fluorescent ELISA using HRP for AMD in spiked samples was 0.02 ng mL−1 (or 0.03 ng mL−1 for the fluorescent ELISA using ALP), which was approximately 10-fold lower than the conventional ELISA (LOD = 0.2 ng mL−1). The analytical properties of our fluorescent ELISA were compared with some reported immunoassays for AMD detection and summarized in Table 1 . The as-developed fluorescence ELISA had a lower LOD than most of the reported strategies. However, a limitation of the CDs-based fluorescence ELISA was that it still need two separation and wash steps comparing with the LFIA.

In summary, a new and highly sensitive CDs-based fluorescent ELISA was developed based on the generic ELISA platform (using commercial HRP/ ALP-labeled secondary antibody, and commercial substrates). More importantly, this assay did not require covalent linkages between the fluorescent probes (CDs) and analyte (or enzymes) compared with other fluorescent immunoassay formats (e.g., FLISA, FRET-based immunoassay) (Goftman et al., 2016; Sidhu, Singh, Garg, & Singh, 2017). The complicated procedures of labeling and surface modification of CDs were avoided using the IFE strategy. Moreover, HRP and ALP are two widely used enzymes in ELISA due to their high catalytic activity, commercial availability and enzyme-labeled antibody. Our developed CDs-based fluorescent ELISA showed good potential application in detecting trace chemical contaminants in foodstuffs.

The specificity of our developed fluorescent ELISA was determined against non-target analytes including six other common antiviral and antibacterial drugs: moroxydine, ribavirin, acyclovir, acetaminophen, enrofloxacin, and sulfamethazine (all 0.5 ng mL−1). The results shown in Fig. S11 demonstrate that only AMD spiked samples (0.5 ng mL−1) had significantly high inhibition rates. These results confirmed the excellent specificity of the developed fluorescent ELISA. The precision of the fluorescent ELISA was determined by analyzing the variations in intra- and inter-assays. Recovery studies for the intra-assay (n = 6) and inter-assay (n = 6) were conducted by measuring samples spiked with three different concentrations of AMD (low, middle and high levels: 0.5, 3 and 10 ng mL−1). As shown in Table 2 (the HRP/ALP-based fluorescent ELISA): the average recoveries for intra-assay ranged from 70.7% to 92.3% with a coefficient of variation (CV%) ranging from 3.8% to 10.5%; the average recoveries for inter-assay ranged from 73.3% to 95.7% with CV ranging from 6.9% to 12.5%. The intra- and inter-assay variations exhibited an acceptable level of precision for AMD quantification.

In summary, we have developed a fluorescent immunoassay with a simple signal generation for the highly sensitive detection of AMD residual in chicken. Four CDs with fluorescence with blue, green, and red emission were synthesized and characterized. After the systematical study and selecting, the CDs with the maximum excitation/emission wavelength at 380 nm/530 nm and 415 nm/525 nm were adopted to the HRP/ALP-triggered IFE. Then, the signal conversion and amplification strategy based on the IFE of CDs was directly incorporated into the HRP/ALP-based conventional ELISA system to construct novel CDs-based fluorescent ELISA using the general and commercial available immunoreagents and substrates. Moreover, the CDs were directly used as the fluorescence probes without the need for labeling or surface modification. The CDs-based fluorescent ELISA had a LOD of 0.02 ng mL−1 for AMD, which was 10-fold lower than that of the conventional ELISA using the same immunoreagents. The CDs-based fluorescent ELISA provide a flexible, extendable and general platform and showed great potential in detecting other trace chemical contaminants in foodstuff.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.31622057) and National Key R&D Program of China (YS2017YFGH000050), EU H2020 EU-China-Safe (727564).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Additional information in this work included the following: Chemicals and materials. Synthesis of carbon dots. The principle of IFE-based fluorescence quenching of CDs using ALP, Fig. S1. FTIR spectra of CDs, Fig. S2. Mechanism of TMB fluorescence, Fig. S3. Fluorescence stability of CDs, Fig. S4-S7. Fluorescence recovery of CDs after ultrafiltration, Fig. S8. Colorimetric assays for detecting HRP/ALP activity, Fig. S9. Influence of methanol and optimization of fluorescent ELISA, Fig. S10. The specificity of fluorescent ELISA Fig. S11. Quantum yields data of CDs, Table S1. Checkerboard and mean recoveries data for fluorescent ELISA, Table S2. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.05.082.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
